Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: AMG Global Investments & Holdings Pte Ltd (in Liquidation)

Facts.Text: AMG Global Investments & Holdings Pte Ltd is a company incorporated in Singapore with objects of trading in metals and providing financial services.
Prior to 23 August 2017, AMG's bank account with Standard Chartered Bank (Singapore) Limited had a zero balance.
On 23 August 2017, a small deposit of US$7.22 was made into AMG's account with Standard Chartered Bank (Singapore) Limited.

On 21 June 2017, AMG and Food & Beverage Cap Co Ltd entered into a discounting and monetizing agreement and memorandum of understanding under which AMG would receive an irrevocable confirmed documentary letter of credit in the sum of US$2,860,000 issued by Kasikornbank Public Company Limited of Bangkok, Thailand.
Under the 21 June 2017 memorandum of understanding, AMG was to pay 75% of the US$2,860,000, amounting to US$2,145,000, to Food & Beverage Cap Co Ltd within three business days after receipt of funds.
Under the 21 June 2017 memorandum of understanding, AMG was to pay 5% of the US$2,860,000, amounting to US$143,000, to White Lotus Cosmetics Limited as a service fee.
Under the 21 June 2017 memorandum of understanding, AMG was to retain 20% of the US$2,860,000, amounting to US$572,000, described as non-recourse payment for AMG.

Under the arrangement documented in the memorandum of understanding, AMG was described as the seller of 1,000 metric tons of aluminium sheets to Food & Beverage Cap Co Ltd and was to receive payment via the documentary letter of credit issued by Kasikornbank.
On 25 August 2017, US$2,764,104.53 was deposited into AMG's Standard Chartered Bank account pursuant to the documentary letter of credit arrangement.
AMG did not supply 1,000 metric tons of aluminium sheets to Food & Beverage Cap Co Ltd after receipt of the documentary letter of credit proceeds.
AMG did not pay US$2,145,000 to Food & Beverage Cap Co Ltd within three business days after receipt of the documentary letter of credit proceeds.
On 22 November 2017, Food & Beverage Cap Co Ltd issued a statutory demand to AMG for US$2,145,000.
On or about 29 August 2017, the balance in AMG's Standard Chartered Bank account fell below US$2,145,000.
After 29 August 2017, further large payments and withdrawals were made from AMG's Standard Chartered Bank account.
AMG appears to have had no assets other than the balance in its Standard Chartered Bank account and no other active source of business revenue during the relevant period.
On 9 March 2018, AMG was wound up based on an unsatisfied statutory demand dated 22 November 2017 in the amount of US$2,145,000.
The petitioning creditor in the winding up of AMG was Food & Beverage Cap Co Ltd, a company incorporated in Thailand.
A liquidator, Abuthahir s/o Abdul Gafoor of AAG Corporate Advisory Pte Ltd, was appointed for AMG and later commenced this action.
A statutory demand dated 22 November 2017 for US$2,145,000 was issued by Food & Beverage Cap Co Ltd to AMG.

The memorandum of understanding dated 21 June 2017 between AMG and Food & Beverage Cap Co Ltd specified payment percentages of 75% to Food & Beverage Cap Co Ltd, 5% to White Lotus Cosmetics Limited, and 20% to AMG from the documentary letter of credit value.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether payments and withdrawals from AMG's bank account and related arrangements concerning a documentary letter of credit and monetizing agreement resulted in financial loss to AMG and its creditors.
Party_Details.Rule: Company directors are generally ensure that company funds are applied consistently with contractual arrangements and that obligations to counterparties and creditors are met when due.
Party_Details.Application: AMG entered into a monetizing arrangement involving a documentary letter of credit for US$2,860,000, received US$2,764,104.53 into its bank account, did not deliver the referenced aluminium sheets, and did not pay the agreed US$2,145,000 to the counterparty while multiple withdrawals and payments reduced the account balance below the amount owed.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judgment analyses directors' duties, causation of loss, insolvency timing, and applies statutory provisions including Companies Act sections 145(5), 157, 169, and 391 to determine the personal monetary liability of the defendants and to reject relief under section 391.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Ong Kee Ming Richard

Facts.Text: Richard Ong Kee Ming is a Singapore citizen.
Richard Ong was a director of AMG Global Investments & Holdings Pte Ltd from 13 September 2006 to 25 September 2017.
From 23 August 2017 to 21 September 2017, Richard Ong and Manu Poovannunilkunnathil Kuttappan were joint signatories to AMG's bank account with Standard Chartered Bank (Singapore) Limited.
On 25 August 2017, Richard Ong countersigned a cash withdrawal of US$11,216.75 from AMG's Standard Chartered Bank account.

On 25 August 2017, Richard Ong countersigned a payment of US$22,556.39 from AMG's Standard Chartered Bank account to ORO Trading Pte Ltd, which was described to him as a repayment of a S$31,000 deposit for a failed gold trade in December 2015 or January 2016.

On 26 August 2017, Richard Ong countersigned a payment of US$9,398.50 from AMG's Standard Chartered Bank account to Lim Chin Keng, which was described to him as payment for air tickets for Manu Poovannunilkunnathil Kuttappan to travel to Africa and India on AMG's business.

On 26 August 2017, Richard Ong countersigned a payment of US$22,142.86 from AMG's Standard Chartered Bank account to Chen Mei Qi, which was described to him as repayment of a personal loan to Manu Poovannunilkunnathil Kuttappan.
On 26 August 2017, Richard Ong countersigned a cash withdrawal of US$20,000 from AMG's Standard Chartered Bank account without receiving supporting documents.
On 28 August 2017, Richard Ong countersigned a payment of US$149,398.22 from AMG's Standard Chartered Bank account to Abdul Razeed after expressing concerns about the size of the payment.

On 28 August 2017, Richard Ong received a payment of US$74,699.11 from AMG's Standard Chartered Bank account, which he understood to be remuneration for his services as a director, including past years' service.
On 28 August 2017, Richard Ong countersigned a cash withdrawal of US$112,666.38 from AMG's Standard Chartered Bank account without documented explanation of purpose.

On 28 August 2017, Richard Ong countersigned a payment of US$5,000 from AMG's Standard Chartered Bank account to an individual named Adanuty, which he understood to be related to repayment of a personal loan of Manu Poovannunilkunnathil Kuttappan.

On 29 August 2017, Richard Ong countersigned two payments from AMG's Standard Chartered Bank account to Sabre Global Pte Ltd totalling part of US$181,119.92, with transfer forms describing them as consultancy fees.
On 5 September 2017, Richard Ong countersigned a further payment from AMG's Standard Chartered Bank account to Sabre Global Pte Ltd, bringing the total of three payments to US$181,119.92.
On 29 August 2017, Richard Ong countersigned a payment of US$75,000 from AMG's Standard Chartered Bank account to Manu Poovannunilkunnathil Kuttappan, which was described as directors' fees.

On 29 August 2017, Richard Ong countersigned a payment of US$100,000 from AMG's Standard Chartered Bank account to Three Quality Services Limited, which was described as consultancy fees on the transfer form.

On 5 September 2017 and 21 September 2017, Richard Ong countersigned payments totalling US$450,000 from AMG's Standard Chartered Bank account to Kevin Anthony Griffin Jr, with transfer forms describing them as relating to the supply of aluminium.

On 5 September 2017, Richard Ong countersigned a payment of US$143,000 from AMG's Standard Chartered Bank account to White Lotus Cosmetics Limited, matching the 5% service fee specified in the 21 June 2017 memorandum of understanding.

On 6 September 2017, Richard Ong countersigned a payment of US$150,000 from AMG's Standard Chartered Bank account to Quality Printers Pte Ltd without knowing the identity of the payee or the underlying transaction.

On 21 September 2017, Richard Ong countersigned a payment of US$37,458.77 from AMG's Standard Chartered Bank account to Koh Ting Giap, which was intended as the first half of a US$75,000 amount connected with Koh Ting Giap rejoining AMG.

On or about 11 August 2017, Richard Ong obtained a handwritten confirmation from Manu Poovannunilkunnathil Kuttappan stating that Manu would take full responsibility for payments made from AMG's Standard Chartered Bank account and for the procurement and supply of goods mentioned in the documentary letter of credit.

Richard Ong signed, together with Manu Poovannunilkunnathil Kuttappan, a security agreement with Standard Chartered Bank establishing a facility secured against a fixed deposit of US$1,000,000 in AMG's name.
A directors' resolution dated 21 September 2017 recorded that 640,000 shares in AMG were registered in the name of Richard Ong at that time.

During late August 2017, Richard Ong told Manu Poovannunilkunnathil Kuttappan that he wanted to step down as a director and expressed that he did not want to continue signing large payment instructions.

Richard Ong attended at Clementi Polyclinic in or around September 2017 and was referred to the National University Hospital for investigation of a possible psychosis episode, according to a clinic memo dated 26 February 2020.
Richard Ong was warded at the Institute of Mental Health for approximately three weeks from 15 October 2017, after his resignation as a director.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether Richard Ong's pre-dispute conduct in countersigning specific withdrawals and payments from AMG's bank account and entering into a banking facility resulted in personal financial responsibility for losses associated with those transactions.
Party_Details.Rule: Company directors are generally act in the interests of the company, to use reasonable care and skill in authorising payments, and to understand and document the purpose of significant transactions, especially where they are joint signatories on bank accounts.
Party_Details.Application: While serving as a director and joint bank signatory, Richard Ong authorised multiple high-value cash withdrawals and transfers from AMG's bank account, including payments to individuals, entities described as consultants, and to himself and another director, in circumstances where explanations and supporting documents were limited or absent and where he had begun expressing concerns about the size and nature of the transactions.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court evaluated Richard Ong's knowledge, timing of his concerns, and the sequence of payments, and concluded that from 28 August 2017 onward he understood or ought to have understood that certain payments were not for proper corporate purposes. The judgment applies fiduciary duty principles and Companies Act provisions to hold him personally liable for specific sums, rejects mental health as a basis for excusing his conduct during the relevant period, and declines relief under section 391 on the basis that his actions were not objectively reasonable.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Koh Ting Giap

Facts.Text: Koh Ting Giap is a Singapore citizen.
Koh Ting Giap was a director of AMG Global Investments & Holdings Pte Ltd from 13 September 2006 to 10 January 2014, and again from 25 September 2017 until AMG was wound up.

After 26 September 2017, Manu Poovannunilkunnathil Kuttappan became the sole signatory of AMG's bank account with Standard Chartered Bank, pursuant to a directors' resolution signed by Koh Ting Giap and Manu.
A directors' resolution dated 25 September 2017 recorded a transfer of 640,000 shares in AMG from Koh Ting Giap to Manu Poovannunilkunnathil Kuttappan.
By 23 January 2018, Manu Poovannunilkunnathil Kuttappan had become the 100% owner of AMG, and Koh Ting Giap no longer held shares in AMG.

In September and October 2006, 1,079,996 shares in AMG were allotted to Koh Ting Giap as a nominee holding on trust for Weeds Natural Remedies Pte Ltd in consideration of Weeds providing formulae for natural health supplements and an eight-year licence to use certain registered trade marks.

Weeds Natural Remedies Pte Ltd provided AMG with health supplement formulae and an eight-year licence to use certain registered trade marks in exchange for share allotments registered in the name of Koh Ting Giap.

In an email dated 13 June 2009 to Manu Poovannunilkunnathil Kuttappan, Koh Ting Giap recorded that his contributions to AMG comprised S$205,000 cash, S$85,000 loan, S$315,000 office expenses, and S$300,000 office renovation, totalling S$905,000.

In an email dated 28 July 2009 to Richard Ong, Koh Ting Giap stated that his total cash and liabilities relating to AMG were S$614,000, comprising S$205,000 cash, S$94,000 loan, and S$315,000 office rental expenses.
Around early 2014, Koh Ting Giap left AMG to focus on his own transactions after AMG had done very little business for years.

In 2017, Manu Poovannunilkunnathil Kuttappan requested that Koh Ting Giap rejoin AMG as the locally resident director after a letter of credit had been monetized, stating that Richard Ong was not cooperating with him.

When rejoining AMG in 2017, Koh Ting Giap agreed that Manu Poovannunilkunnathil Kuttappan would be the 100% shareholder and sole bank account signatory and would take full responsibility for AMG's matters.
Koh Ting Giap rejoined AMG in 2017 with the understanding that AMG would repay part of what he regarded as loans and expenses he had previously provided to AMG.
On 21 September 2017, AMG paid US$37,458.77 to Koh Ting Giap from its Standard Chartered Bank account, intended as the first half of a US$75,000 amount connected with his rejoining AMG.
On 29 September 2017, AMG paid US$37,247.43 to Koh Ting Giap from its Standard Chartered Bank account, which he regarded as partial repayment of his loan to AMG.

Between late September 2017 and 2018, twelve cash withdrawals or cash cheques totalling US$148,064.97 were made from AMG's Standard Chartered Bank account while Koh Ting Giap was a director and Manu Poovannunilkunnathil Kuttappan was sole signatory.
On 2 October 2017, AMG paid US$10,000 from its Standard Chartered Bank account to Uniroyal Communications Pte Ltd without contemporaneous supporting documentation in AMG's records.

On 2 October 2017, AMG paid US$11,000 from its Standard Chartered Bank account to Chua Pair Shen; an acknowledgement dated 3 August 2017 signed by Manu Poovannunilkunnathil Kuttappan described S$15,000 as a loan to AMG from this person.
On 3 October 2017, AMG paid US$11,500 from its Standard Chartered Bank account to Shuan Jarmen without supporting documents in AMG's records.
On 11 October 2017, AMG paid US$3,700 from its Standard Chartered Bank account to Christina Marie Nyanmani without supporting documents in AMG's records.
AMG made a payment of US$1,370 from its Standard Chartered Bank account to Ng Wai Loon without supporting documents in AMG's records.

AMG made a payment of US$1,850 from its Standard Chartered Bank account to Abdul Razeed without supporting documents in AMG's records during the period when Koh Ting Giap was a director and Manu was sole signatory.

On 26 September 2017, a directors' resolution signed by Koh Ting Giap and Manu Poovannunilkunnathil Kuttappan authorised Manu to be the sole signatory for AMG's banking facilities with Standard Chartered Bank.

On or about 9 November 2017, AMG drew down US$744,000 under its Standard Chartered Bank facility with Standard Chartered Bank and paid this amount to Smart Recycling Pte Ltd, supported by an invoice and delivery order from Smart Recycling Pte Ltd.
Interest of US$10,879.76 accrued on the drawdown of US$744,000 under AMG's Standard Chartered Bank facility after the payment to Smart Recycling Pte Ltd.

When invited to rejoin AMG in 2017, Koh Ting Giap understood from Manu Poovannunilkunnathil Kuttappan that Richard Ong had been reluctant to sign cheques and that this reluctance made it difficult for Manu to carry out transactions.
After rejoining AMG in 2017, Koh Ting Giap met frequently with Manu Poovannunilkunnathil Kuttappan to discuss AMG's business and sought repayment of amounts he regarded as loans to AMG.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether Koh Ting Giap's pre-dispute conduct in agreeing to Manu Poovannunilkunnathil Kuttappan being sole bank signatory, in receiving payments from AMG, and in connection with withdrawals and facility drawdowns resulted in personal financial responsibility for losses associated with those transactions.
Party_Details.Rule: Company directors are generally safeguard company assets, to implement or maintain reasonable controls over bank accounts, and to consider the financial position of the company, including creditor interests, when authorising or enabling payments and facility drawdowns.
Party_Details.Application: After rejoining AMG as director, Koh Ting Giap signed a resolution allowing Manu Poovannunilkunnathil Kuttappan to be sole signatory on AMG's bank account despite knowing that Richard Ong had previously been reluctant to sign certain cheques, received two payments from AMG that he regarded as partial loan repayments, and did not put in place alternative checks while multiple undocumented withdrawals and a US$744,000 facility drawdown to Smart Recycling Pte Ltd occurred.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court found that Koh Ting Giap rejoined AMG primarily to recover sums he believed were owed to him, knowingly allowed Manu to operate the bank account without checks despite indications of prior disagreements with Richard Ong over payments, and thereby facilitated withdrawals and a facility drawdown that were not for AMG's purposes. Applying fiduciary duty principles, insolvency-related creditor interest considerations, and knowing receipt doctrine for the pre-directorship payment, the court held him personally liable for specified sums and declined to grant relief under Companies Act section 391 on the basis that he had not acted honestly or reasonably.
